title: Green: Quick Build Guide
content:
Green: Quick Build Guide
You are building something quick — an internal tool, a prototype, or a throwaway. This guide walks you through it step by step.
Time estimate: Most people finish a Green build in 1-4 hours depending on complexity. The framework part takes about 15 minutes.
What you will need: Access to an AI coding tool (see Step 2) and a clear idea of what you want to build.
---
Step 1: Define what you are building (5 min)
Fill out the Project Brief. This is a short form that captures what you are building, why, and what "done" looks like.
Example completed brief:
> Project name: Team Directory
>
> What are you building? A simple web page that shows everyone on our team — their name, role, and contact info. Used by new joiners to find who does what.
>
> What problem does it solve? New team members currently ask Slack "who handles X?" multiple times a week. A directory saves everyone time.
>
> What does "done" look like?
> 1. Shows all team members with name, role, email, and photo
> 2. Searchable by name or role
> 3. Works on mobile
>
> Tools: Lovable (fast for simple pages, no backend needed)
Accelerator
Before you start building, check with your team — someone may have already built something similar. A 2-minute Slack message could save you hours of work.
---
Step 2: Pick your tool
For a Quick Build, almost any AI tool works. Pick what you are most comfortable with.
Never used an AI coding tool before? Check the Tool Selection Guide for an overview, then pick one from your company's Approved Tools list.
Quick rule of thumb for Green builds:
- Want it done in minutes with no code? → No-Code tools (Lovable, Bolt, v0)
- Want to see and tweak the code? → Low-Code tools (Replit Agent)
- Already comfortable coding? → Pro Tools (Cursor, Claude Code)
Any of these will work for a Green build. Do not overthink it.
---
Step 3: Build it
Open your AI tool and describe what you want. The more specific your prompt, the better the result.
Accelerator
Start from the Promp

title: Orange: Reviewed Build Guide
content:
Orange: Reviewed Build Guide
You are building something that handles personal data or serves external users — a parent communication portal, a client-facing tool, or an internal system with sensitive information. This guide adds data protection, accessibility, and technical review on top of the Standard Build process.
Time estimate: Most Reviewed Builds take 3-7 days of building time. The framework part takes about 1-2 hours.
What you will need: Access to a Low-Code or Pro AI tool, your company's Branding Standard, a colleague for testing, and a technical reviewer (see Roles).
---
Steps 1-3: Standard Build Foundations
Follow the Yellow (Standard Build) Guide for Steps 1-3. The process is the same:
1. Define what you are building — fill out the Project Brief
2. Pick your tool — for Orange builds, Low-Code or Pro Tools are recommended. No-Code tools usually lack the security controls you need.
3. Build it — use the Prompt Library as a starting point. Keep a Build Log.
One important difference: For Orange builds, your Project Brief should explicitly state what personal data you are collecting and why.
---
Step 4: Handle data correctly
This is where Orange builds differ most from Yellow. You are handling personal data, and that comes with responsibilities.
4a: Minimize data collection
For every piece of data your tool collects, ask: "Is this strictly necessary for the tool to work?"
If the answer is no, do not collect it. Data you do not have cannot be stolen.
Example:
| Data field | Necessary? | Decision |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Parent email | Yes — needed for communication | Keep |
| Parent date of birth | No — not used by the tool | Remove |
| Child's full name | Yes — needed to match to records | Keep |
| Child's photo | No — nice to have but not essential | Remove |
4b: Encrypt data
- In transit: Your tool must use HTTPS. Check for the lock icon in your browser's address bar.
- At rest: Your database must encrypt stored data. Most modern services (S

title: Red: Protected Build Guide
content:
Red: Protected Build Guide
You are building something that handles protected data, serves minors, or has compliance requirements — a student-facing tool, a health data portal, or a system handling legally protected information. This guide adds privacy compliance, incident response planning, and designated reviewer sign-off on top of the Reviewed Build process.
Time estimate: Most Protected Builds take 1-3 weeks of building time. The framework part takes 2-4 hours spread across the process.
What you will need: A Pro AI tool (recommended), your company's Branding Standard, test users representative of your actual audience, a technical reviewer, and a designated reviewer (see Roles).
Important: The Red path is designed to feel like maximum support, not maximum punishment. Every step exists to protect your users and your organization. If any step feels unclear, see When to Escalate.
---
Steps 1-3: Standard Build Foundations
Follow the Yellow (Standard Build) Guide for the basics:
1. Define what you are building — fill out the Project Brief. For Red builds, explicitly state: what protected data you collect, who your users are (especially if they include minors), and what compliance requirements apply.
2. Pick your tool — Pro Tools (Cursor, Claude Code) are strongly recommended. You need full control over security, data handling, and infrastructure.
3. Build it — start from the Prompt Library. The "Student-facing content page" prompt includes safety and privacy constraints. Keep a detailed Build Log.
---
Steps 4-6: Reviewed Build Additions
Follow the Orange (Reviewed Build) Guide for:
4. Handle data correctly — minimize collection, encrypt at rest and in transit, verify access controls, list third-party services
5. Make it accessible — keyboard navigation, text contrast, alt text, mobile support
6. Handle errors gracefully — user-friendly messages, no technical details exposed
These steps are the same as Orange but more critical for Red builds. Take extra care with each o

title: Yellow: Standard Build Guide
content:
Yellow: Standard Build Guide
You are building something your team will use for real work — a dashboard, a workflow tool, or a data entry system. It handles business data and needs to look professional. This guide walks you through it.
Time estimate: Most Standard Builds take 1-3 days of building time. The framework part takes about 30 minutes.
What you will need: Access to an AI coding tool, your company's Branding Standard, and a colleague willing to test your tool.
---
Step 1: Define what you are building (10 min)
Fill out the Project Brief. For a Standard Build, be thorough — this tool will be used for real work, so "done" criteria matter.
Example completed brief:
> Project name: Team Performance Dashboard
>
> What are you building? A dashboard showing weekly team metrics — tasks completed, response times, and satisfaction scores. Used by team leads during Monday standups.
>
> What problem does it solve? Team leads currently pull data from three spreadsheets manually. Takes 30 minutes per week and the numbers are sometimes wrong.
>
> What does "done" look like?
> 1. Shows tasks completed per person for the past 4 weeks
> 2. Shows average response time with trend arrows
> 3. Shows satisfaction score from the latest survey
> 4. Filters by team and date range
> 5. Matches company branding (colors, logo, fonts)
>
> Tools: Replit Agent (needs a backend to pull data from multiple sources)
---
Step 2: Pick your tool
For a Standard Build, you need a tool that lets you see and edit the code, because you may need to customize beyond what AI generates.
Recommended for Yellow builds:
- Low-Code (Replit Agent) — good balance of speed and control, handles databases and APIs
- Pro Tools (Cursor, Claude Code) — maximum control, best for complex logic
Not recommended for Yellow builds:
- No-Code tools are fast but hard to customize and harder to move away from. If this tool will be used for months, consider starting with Low-Code or Pro.
See the Tool Selection Guide for a full co

title: Green: Quick Build Checklist
content:
Green: Quick Build Checklist
Your build path: Quick Build (internal use, no sensitive data, throwaway or replaceable)
Complete every item before going live. Expand any item for details.
---
Pre-Build
    
Even a quick build needs a sentence describing what it does and what "done" looks like. If you cannot describe it, you are not ready to build it.
During Build
    
If your laptop dies, can you get your work back? Save to a cloud tool (Replit, GitHub, Google Drive) — not just your desktop.
    
"test123" will not reveal problems. Use realistic names, numbers, and edge cases (empty fields, very long text, special characters like accents or apostrophes).
    
1. Try entering a name with an apostrophe (e.g., "O'Brien")
    2. Try entering an empty form — does it handle it?
    3. Try entering a very long string (200+ characters)
    4. Try entering special characters (accents, emoji, symbols)
    5. If any of these cause a crash or blank screen, fix it before shipping
Pre-Ship
    
Open your project brief. Walk through each item under "What does done look like?" Does the tool do all of them? Not some — all.
    
Anyone who sees your code can use your accounts and access your data. This is the most common security mistake in AI-assisted development.
    
1. Search your code for: , , , , , 
    2. Search for any long random string that looks like a key
    3. If you find any, move them to environment variables (your tool's settings or secrets panel)
    4. Not sure how? See When to Escalate
    
You need a kill switch. "I can delete the deployment" counts. "I can set it to private" counts. Know how to do it before you need to.
---
All checked? You are good to go.
Your tool starts as Green, but if things change — it handles new data, gets new users, or becomes more critical — re-run the Project Intake to check if your path has changed.

title: Orange: Reviewed Build Checklist
content:
Orange: Reviewed Build Checklist
Your build path: Reviewed Build (personal data, external users, or significant business impact)
Complete every item before going live. Expand any item for details.
---
Items 1-6: Quick Build Foundations
These are the same items from the Green Checklist.
Pre-Build
    
A Reviewed Build affects real people outside your team. The brief ensures everyone agrees on scope, audience, and success criteria before you invest significant effort.
During Build
    
Save to a version-controlled repository (GitHub, GitLab) — not just a cloud folder. You need the ability to roll back changes.
    
Problems appear with real data. Test with names containing apostrophes, accents, and hyphens. Test with empty fields, very long text, and special characters.
    
1. Test with names: O'Brien, Müller, Smith-Jones, 日本語の名前
    2. Test with empty forms, maximum-length inputs, and special characters
    3. Test with numbers: zero, negative, very large, decimal
    4. Test on different browsers (Chrome, Safari, Firefox)
    5. Test on mobile devices
Pre-Ship
    
Walk through every "done" criterion in your project brief. Every single one must work.
    
With external users, exposed secrets are a direct path to data breaches.
    
1. Search your code for: , , , , , 
    2. Check your repository history — secrets removed from code may still be in git history
    3. If you find any, move them to environment variables and rotate the exposed secrets (get new ones)
    
For a Reviewed Build, the kill switch should be fast (under 5 minutes) and documented. You may need to use it urgently.
---
Items 7-10: Standard Build Additions
These are the same items from the Yellow Checklist.
    
A fresh pair of eyes catches problems you cannot see because you built it.
    Saved in a Build Log or  file alongside the project.
    
If someone needs to modify this tool, they need the original prompts.
    See the Branding Standard.
    
External users see this as a company product. I

title: Red: Protected Build Checklist
content:
Red: Protected Build Checklist
Your build path: Protected Build (protected data, minors, compliance requirements, or high-stakes)
Complete every item before going live. This checklist requires sign-off from your designated reviewer. Expand any item for details.
---
Items 1-6: Quick Build Foundations
These are the same items from the Green Checklist.
Pre-Build
    
A Protected Build has the highest stakes. The brief must be thorough — explicitly state what protected data you are handling, who your users are, and what compliance requirements apply.
During Build
    
Use version-controlled repositories with access controls. You must be able to track every change and roll back if needed.
    
Test with data representative of your actual users. If your users are students aged 14-18, test with inputs typical of that age group.
    
1. Test with names, characters, and input patterns your actual users would produce
    2. Test with empty fields, maximum-length inputs, and special characters
    3. Test on the devices your users actually have (not just your development machine)
    4. Test with data volumes you expect in production
Pre-Ship
    
Walk through every "done" criterion. For protected builds, partial delivery is not acceptable.
    
Exposed secrets in a protected build can lead to a data breach affecting vulnerable users.
    
1. Search your code for: , , , , , 
    2. Search your git history for accidentally committed secrets
    3. If any were ever committed, rotate them immediately (get new keys/passwords)
    4. Use a secrets scanner if available
    
The kill switch must be documented, tested, and known to at least two people. Document it in your incident response plan.
---
Items 7-10: Standard Build Additions
These are the same items from the Yellow Checklist.
    
Fresh eyes catch problems you cannot see.
    
Full prompt documentation is essential for auditing and maintenance.
    
Users interacting with protected data need to trust that the tool is legiti

title: Yellow: Standard Build Checklist
content:
Yellow: Standard Build Checklist
Your build path: Standard Build (business data, moderate customization, ongoing use)
Complete every item before going live. Expand any item for details.
---
Items 1-6: Quick Build Foundations
These are the same items from the Green Checklist. Every Yellow build must meet these first.
Pre-Build
    
A Standard Build is not a throwaway — it will be used by your team for real work. The brief ensures everyone agrees on what "done" means before you invest significant time.
During Build
    
If your laptop dies, can you get your work back? Save to a cloud tool (Replit, GitHub, Google Drive) — not just your desktop.
    
Problems appear with real data, not test data. A name like "O'Brien" or a 200-character address will break things that "John Smith" never will.
    
1. Try entering names with apostrophes, accents, and hyphens (O'Brien, Müller, Smith-Jones)
    2. Try entering empty fields — does the tool handle them?
    3. Try very long text (200+ characters)
    4. Try special characters and emoji
    5. If your tool handles numbers, try zero, negative numbers, and very large numbers
Pre-Ship
    
Open your project brief. Walk through each item under "What does done look like?" Does the tool do all of them?
    
Anyone who sees your code can use your accounts and access your data.
    
1. Search your code for: , , , , , 
    2. Search for any long random string that looks like a key
    3. If you find any, move them to environment variables
    4. Not sure how? See When to Escalate
    
You need a kill switch. Know how to take the tool offline before you need to.
---
Items 7-10: Standard Build Additions
These items are specific to Yellow builds and above.
    Not "looked at" — actually used it. Asked them to complete the main task without help. Watched where they got confused.
    
You already know how your tool works. A fresh pair of eyes will find problems you cannot see because you built it. This is the single most effective quality c

title: Approved Tools
content:
Approved Tools
This document lists the AI coding tools your company has approved for use, and what build paths each tool is approved for.
Search this document for  to find all fields that need your input.
---
AI coding tools approved for use
[CLIENT: Fill in this table with your approved tools. Add or remove rows as needed.]
| Tool | Tier | Approved for | Not approved for | Notes |
|------|------|-------------|------------------|-------|
| [CLIENT: e.g., Lovable] | No-Code | Green builds | Yellow+ builds | [CLIENT: reason, e.g., "No code export, limited data handling"] |
| [CLIENT: e.g., Replit Agent] | Low-Code | Green, Yellow builds | Orange, Red builds | [CLIENT: reason, e.g., "Data residency not confirmed for EU compliance"] |
| [CLIENT: e.g., Cursor] | Pro Tool | All build paths | — | [CLIENT: reason, e.g., "Full code control, approved by security team"] |
| [CLIENT: e.g., Claude Code] | Pro Tool | All build paths | — | [CLIENT: reason] |
See the Tool Selection Guide for a full explanation of No-Code, Low-Code, and Pro Tool tiers, including strengths, limitations, and when to use each.
---
Other approved services
[CLIENT: List any other services your team is approved to use with AI-built tools.]
| Service | Type | Approved for | Notes |
|---------|------|-------------|-------|
| [CLIENT: e.g., Supabase] | Database | All build paths | [CLIENT: notes] |
| [CLIENT: e.g., Vercel] | Hosting | Green, Yellow, Orange | [CLIENT: notes] |
| [CLIENT: e.g., OpenAI API] | AI API | Orange, Red (with review) | [CLIENT: notes] |
| [CLIENT: e.g., Google Analytics] | Analytics | Green, Yellow only | [CLIENT: "Not approved for tools serving minors"] |
---
Requesting a new tool
If you want to use a tool that is not on this list:
1. [CLIENT: Describe the process — e.g., "Submit a request to the IT team using the internal tool request form"]
2. [CLIENT: Who approves — e.g., "IT Security reviews and approves within 5 business days"]
3. [CLIENT: Criteria — e.g., "The tool must meet ou

title: Company Context
content:
Company Context
This document captures your company's specific values, constraints, and policies that affect how AI tools are built. Fill in each section before rolling out VCF to your team.
Search this document for  to find all fields that need your input.
---
About your company
Company name: [CLIENT: Your company name]
Industry: [CLIENT: e.g., Education, Healthcare, Financial Services, Non-profit]
Size: [CLIENT: e.g., 50 employees, 500 employees, 5000 employees]
Primary AI use cases: [CLIENT: What does your company want to use AI-built tools for? e.g., "Internal dashboards, student engagement tracking, automated reporting, parent communication tools"]
---
Company values that affect how we build
[CLIENT: What principles guide your work? These values will be referenced throughout the framework when making build decisions.]
Examples:
- "Student safety comes before speed of delivery"
- "Every tool must be accessible to users with disabilities"
- "We move fast on internal tools but carefully on anything student-facing"
- "Data minimization — we only collect what we strictly need"
Your values:

title: Prompt Library
content:
Prompt Library
Tested prompts for common build tasks. Start from these instead of writing from scratch — they produce reliable results and follow the framework's standards.
For guidance on writing and managing prompts, see the Prompt Management Standard.
---
How to use these prompts
1. Find a prompt below that matches what you are building
2. Copy it into your AI tool
3. Replace the  placeholders with your specific details
4. Adjust as needed for your project
These are starting points, not rigid templates. Modify them to fit your needs.
---
Internal dashboard
Use this when building a dashboard that shows metrics or data summaries for internal staff.
---
Data entry form
Use this when building a form that collects and validates user input.
---
Branded landing page
Use this when building a page that represents your company externally.
---
Student-facing content page (Red path)
Use this for any tool or page that students will interact with. Includes safety and privacy constraints.
---
How to add to this library
Built a great prompt? Share it with your team:
1. Document it using the Prompt Spec Template
2. Test it at least 3 times with different inputs
3. Submit it to the Framework contact for inclusion
[CLIENT: Add your company-specific prompts below this line]
---
Related documents
- Prompt Management Standard — rules for build and production prompts
- Prompt Spec Template — detailed template for documenting production prompts
- Branding Standard — colors, fonts, and tone of voice to use in prompts

title: Roles and Contacts
content:
Roles and Contacts
This document defines who does what in the VCF framework — who reviews builds, who approves tools, and who to contact when something goes wrong.
Search this document for  to find all fields that need your input.
---
Framework roles
| Role | Person | Contact | Responsibilities |
|------|--------|---------|------------------|
| Peer reviewer | Any team member | — | Reviews Yellow builds. Checks that the builder followed the checklist and the tool works as described. |
| Technical reviewer | [CLIENT: Name] | [CLIENT: Email] | Reviews Orange builds. Checks security, data handling, access controls, and technical architecture. |
| Designated reviewer | [CLIENT: Name] | [CLIENT: Email] | Signs off Red builds. Verifies compliance, privacy impact, and age-appropriate design. Has authority to approve or block launch. |
| Framework contact | [CLIENT: Name] | [CLIENT: Email] | Answers questions about VCF itself. Updates framework documents. Runs training sessions. |
| Emergency contact | [CLIENT: Name] | [CLIENT: Phone] | First call for critical incidents (data exposure, safety risk, compliance breach). Available outside business hours. |
---
Who reviews what
| Build path | Reviewer role | What they check |
|------------|--------------|-----------------|
| Green (Quick Build) | No formal review required | Builder self-checks using the Green Checklist |
| Yellow (Standard Build) | Peer reviewer | Checklist completion, basic functionality, realistic data testing |
| Orange (Reviewed Build) | Technical reviewer | Security, data handling, branding, access controls, third-party services |
| Red (Protected Build) | Designated reviewer | Everything above, plus compliance, privacy impact, age-appropriate design, incident response plan |
Green builds are low-risk — a self-check is enough. As risk increases, you need reviewers with more expertise. A peer can check if a tool works correctly. A technical reviewer can check if it is secure. A designated reviewer can check

title: Build Log
content:
Build Log
Keep a running log of decisions and changes during your build. This is your project diary — when someone asks "why did you do it that way?", this log has the answer.
Required for Yellow+ builds. Recommended for all builds.
---
Project name: ___
Builder: ___
Build path: ___
Started: ___
---
How to use this log
Copy the entry template below for each work session. Date each entry. Focus on decisions and problems, not just what you did.
Three reasons:
1. Future you — in two months, you will not remember why you chose Chart.js over D3, or why the dashboard has 4 tabs instead of 5.
2. Handover — if someone else needs to modify your tool, the build log tells them what you tried, what worked, and what did not.
3. Prompt preservation — AI prompts that produced good results are valuable. Save them here so you (or others) can reuse them.
---
Entry template
Copy everything below the line for each new entry.
---
Date: ___
What I did:
[Brief description of work done this session]
Decisions made:
[Any choices you made and why. Be specific.]
> Example: "Used Chart.js instead of D3 because Chart.js was simpler for basic line charts and had better documentation for beginners."
AI prompts used:
[Paste or summarize the prompts you gave the AI tool. Include the tool name.]
> Example: "Replit Agent — 'Create a line chart showing daily attendance percentage for the last 30 days. Use Chart.js. Data comes from the /api/attendance endpoint. Use the company colors: primary #1B3A5C, accent #2ABFBF.'"
Issues encountered:
[Anything that went wrong, surprised you, or took longer than expected]
Next steps:
[What you plan to do in the next session]
---
Tips for effective entries
- Be honest about problems. "The AI generated code that crashed when the database was empty" is useful. "Everything went fine" is not.
- Save your prompts. Especially the ones that worked well. They save time when building similar features later.
- Note what you changed from AI output. If the AI generated somethin

title: Incident Report
content:
Incident Report
Fill this out when something goes wrong with a live tool. The sooner you document what happened, the faster it gets resolved.
If you are unsure whether something counts as an incident, see When to Escalate.
---
Tool name: ___
Reported by: ___
Date and time discovered: ___
---
Severity
Check one:
Ask yourself:
1. Is anyone's personal data exposed or at risk? → Critical
2. Are minors affected? → Critical
3. Could this cause legal or compliance problems? → Critical
4. Are users blocked with no workaround? → High
5. Can users work around it? → Medium
6. Is it just a visual or cosmetic issue? → Low
When in doubt, choose a higher severity. It is easier to downgrade than to explain why you underreported.
---
What happened?
Describe what went wrong in plain language. Include what users saw, when it started, and how it was discovered.
---
Who is affected?
- User type: ___ (internal staff / external users / students / parents)
- Approximate number affected: ___
- Are minors affected? Yes / No / Unknown
---
What did you do immediately?
List the steps you took as soon as you discovered the issue. If you turned the tool off, say so.
1. ___
2. ___
3. ___
---
Root cause (if known)
What caused this? Check any that apply:
Details: ___
---
What needs to happen next?
| Action | Who | By when |
|--------|-----|---------|
| ___ | ___ | ___ |
| ___ | ___ | ___ |
| ___ | ___ | ___ |
---
Notifications
Who has been told about this incident?
| Person / Team | How notified | When |
|---------------|-------------|------|
| ___ | ___ | ___ |
| ___ | ___ | ___ |
---
Lessons learned (fill in after resolution)
Date resolved: ___
What would prevent this from happening again?
- ___
- ___
Changes made as a result:
- ___
- ___
---
Related documents
- When to Escalate
- Security Standard
- Roles and Contacts

title: Project Brief
content:
Project Brief
Fill this out before you start building. This document captures what you are building, why, and how you will know it is done.
---
Project name: ___
Builder: ___ (your name)
Date: ___
Build path: ___ (from your Project Intake result)
---
What are you building?
Describe in 2-3 sentences what this tool does and who it is for.
> Example: "A dashboard that shows daily attendance data for Year 7-9 students. Used by heads of year to spot patterns and follow up with students who have irregular attendance."
---
What problem does it solve?
Why does this tool need to exist? What is the current pain point?
> Example: "Right now, attendance data lives in three spreadsheets that are updated manually. Heads of year spend 2 hours a week merging data before they can spot trends."
---
What does "done" look like?
List 3-5 specific things this tool must do to be complete. Be as concrete as possible — these become your acceptance criteria.
1. ___
2. ___
3. ___
4. ___ (optional)
5. ___ (optional)
> Example:
> 1. Shows daily attendance percentage per student for the last 30 days
> 2. Highlights students below 90% attendance in red
> 3. Allows filtering by year group and form
> 4. Exports filtered view as CSV
---
What tools will you use?
Which AI tool(s) will you use to build this? See the Tool Selection Guide for help choosing.
Tool: ___
Why this tool: ___
---
Who needs to know about this?
List anyone who should review this project, will be affected by it, or needs to approve it before it goes live.
| Person | Role | Why they need to know |
|--------|------|-----------------------|
| ___ | ___ | ___ |
| ___ | ___ | ___ |
---
Target go-live date: ___
---
What happens next
1. Complete the Project Intake to determine your build path (if you have not already)
2. Read the build guide for your path: Green, Yellow, Orange, or Red
3. Start building, using the Build Log to track progress
4. When ready, submit a Review Request (Yellow+ builds)

title: Prompt Specification
content:
Prompt Specification
Use this template for every production prompt in your tool — any prompt that runs inside the finished tool and affects what users see.
For background on why production prompts need documentation, see the Prompt Management Standard.
---
Prompt name: ___
Tool or feature it belongs to: ___
Author: ___
Last updated: ___
Version: ___ (increment each time you change the prompt text)
---
What does this prompt do?
One sentence: This prompt tells the AI to ___.
> Example: "This prompt tells the AI to summarize a student's weekly progress into 3 bullet points suitable for parent communication."
---
The prompt text
---
Expected input
What kind of data does this prompt receive? Include 1-2 example inputs.
Input type: ___ (e.g., free text, structured data, user question)
Example input 1:
Example input 2:
---
Expected output
What should the AI produce? Include 1-2 example outputs.
Output format: ___ (e.g., paragraph, bullet points, JSON, single sentence)
Example output 1:
Example output 2:
---
Known limitations
When does this prompt give bad results? What inputs cause problems?
- ___

- ___

title: Review Request
content:
Review Request
Fill this out when your build is ready for review. Submit it to your reviewer along with access to the tool.
---
Project name: ___
Builder: ___
Build path: ___ (Green / Yellow / Orange / Red)
Reviewer: ___ (see Roles for who reviews your path)
Date submitted: ___
---
What to review
Link to the tool: ___
Brief description: What does this tool do, in 1-2 sentences?
---
Checklist status
---
Items I could not complete or was not sure about
List any checklist items you skipped, could not complete, or were unsure about. Explain why for each.
| Checklist item | Status | Reason |
|----------------|--------|--------|
| ___ | Skipped / Unsure | ___ |
| ___ | Skipped / Unsure | ___ |
If all items are complete, write "All checklist items complete" and delete the table.
---
Specific areas I want feedback on
What are you most uncertain about? What would you like the reviewer to pay special attention to?
1. ___
2. ___
3. ___
---
Supporting documents
Link any relevant documents:
---
Deadline for review: ___
---
For the reviewer
After reviewing, respond with one of:
- Approved — ready to go live
- Approved with notes — can go live, but address these items soon
- Changes needed — must address these items before going live
- Escalate — needs review by someone with more expertise (see Roles)

title: Your First Build
content:
Your First Build
This walkthrough takes you through building a simple tool using the Vibe Coding Framework from start to finish. By the end, you will have a working tool and know how the framework works.
What you will build: A team directory page — a simple web page showing your team members' names, roles, and contact information.
Time: About 30-60 minutes total, including the framework steps.
---
Step 1: Classify your project (3 min)
Open the Project Intake and answer the 5 questions. For our team directory, the answers would be:
> Q1: Who will use this tool? → Just me or my immediate team (internal)
>
> Q2: What kind of data will this tool handle? → Non-sensitive internal data (team names, roles, emails)
>
> Q3: What happens if this tool breaks or gives wrong information? → Minor inconvenience — we can work around it
>
> Q4: How long will this tool be used? → A few weeks/months, or I am not sure
>
> Q5: Does this tool use AI to generate content that users see? → No
Result: All answers point to Green: Quick Build. That is your build path.
---
Step 2: Understand your build path (2 min)
The intake told you this is a Green: Quick Build. That means:
- Low risk — internal users, no sensitive data
- No formal review required — you self-check using a short checklist
- You can use any AI coding tool you are comfortable with
The framework has four paths (Green → Yellow → Orange → Red), each adding more safeguards. Green is the simplest. You will only need the others when your tool handles more sensitive data or serves more critical audiences.
---
Step 3: Fill out the Project Brief (5 min)
Open the Project Brief and fill it in. For our team directory:
> Project name: Team Directory
>
> Builder: [Your name]
>
> Date: [Today's date]
>
> Build path: Green (Quick Build)
>
> What are you building? A simple web page that shows everyone on our team — their name, role, and contact info. Used by new joiners to find who does what.
>
> What problem does it solve? New team members ask "

title: Glossary
content:
Glossary
Plain-language definitions for terms used in the Vibe Coding Framework. If a term is not here, check the homepage or contact the Framework contact.
---
Accessibility
Making a tool usable by people with disabilities — including those who use screen readers, keyboard-only navigation, or have limited vision. Required for Orange+ builds. See the Quality Standard.
API key
A password-like string that lets your tool connect to an external service (like an AI model or database). Must never be visible in your code. Store it in environment variables. See the Security Standard.
Build log
A running record of what you did during a build — decisions made, prompts used, issues encountered. Required for Yellow+ builds. See the Build Log template.
Build path
One of four levels (Green, Yellow, Orange, Red) that determines how much process your project needs. Determined by the Project Intake.
Build prompt
A prompt you type into an AI tool while building your project. These are your instructions to the AI during development. They are temporary — they do not run inside the finished tool. See the Prompt Management Standard.
COPPA
Children's Online Privacy Protection Act. A US law that restricts how websites and tools can collect data from children under 13. Relevant for Red builds serving young users. See the Security Standard.
Designated reviewer
The person who signs off on Red (Protected Build) projects. They have the authority and expertise to evaluate compliance and safety. See Roles.
Encryption
Scrambling data so that only authorized people can read it. "At rest" means when stored in a database. "In transit" means when sent between the user's browser and your server. Required for Orange+ builds handling personal data.
Environment variable
A setting stored outside your code that your tool can read at runtime. Used for API keys, database URLs, and other secrets. The safe alternative to putting secrets in your code.
Escalation
Asking for help from someone with more expertise o

title: Tool Selection Guide
content:
Tool Selection Guide
Choosing the right AI coding tool is one of the first decisions you make. This guide helps you pick a tool that matches your project's needs, your skill level, and your build path.
---
The principle: start low, escalate if needed
Pick the simplest tool that meets your requirements. If you outgrow it, you can move to a more powerful tool later. Starting with a complex tool when a simple one would work wastes time and increases risk.
---
Three tiers of tools
No-Code tools (describe what you want in words)
Tools like Lovable, Bolt, and v0 let you describe what you want in plain language and generate a working tool without writing code.
| Strength | Limitation |
|----------|-----------|
| Fastest way to get a working prototype | Limited customization once generated |
| No coding knowledge needed | Harder to fix when something goes wrong |
| Great for landing pages, simple forms, static sites | Limited data handling and backend logic |
| Visual editing after generation | Vendor lock-in — your project lives on their platform |
Best for: Green builds, prototypes, landing pages, simple internal tools.
If your project is a quick internal tool or prototype with no sensitive data, No-Code tools get you from idea to working tool in minutes, not hours.
---
Low-Code tools (guided coding with AI assistance)
Tools like Replit Agent provide an AI-assisted development environment where you describe what you want and the AI generates code you can see and modify.
| Strength | Limitation |
|----------|-----------|
| Full code visibility — you can see and edit what was generated | Requires basic comfort with code (not writing it, but reading it) |
| Built-in hosting, databases, and deployment | Performance limits at scale |
| Good balance of speed and control | Moderate vendor lock-in |
| Environment variables for secrets management | Some features change frequently |
Best for: Green and Yellow builds, internal dashboards, data entry tools, workflow automations.
Repl

title: When to Escalate
content:
When to Escalate
This is not a failure page.
If you are here, you are doing exactly the right thing. Asking for help early prevents problems. Not asking creates them.
---
Common situations
"I do not know which build path my project is."
What to do: Re-run the Project Intake. Answer each question honestly based on what you know right now. If you are still unsure after completing it, message the Framework contact.
Rule of thumb: If you are unsure, pick the higher path. It is easier to scale back than to realize you under-classified after launch.
---
"I am stuck on a checklist item."
What to do: Skip it and note it in your Review Request. Your reviewer will help you resolve it. Skipping an item and documenting why is much better than getting stuck and stopping altogether.
Example:
> Checklist item: "Personal data is encrypted at rest"
>
> My note: "I am using Supabase and I think it encrypts by default, but I could not confirm this. Flagging for reviewer to verify."
That is a perfectly good response. Your reviewer exists for exactly this reason.
---
"I think there might be a security issue."
What to do: Stop building. Contact the Technical reviewer immediately.
Do not try to fix it yourself. Do not Google it and hope for the best. Security issues are one area where expert help is always the right call. Better to ask and be wrong than to stay silent and be right.
Examples of things that should trigger this:
- You accidentally committed an API key or password to your code
- You noticed users can see other users' data
- Your tool sends data to a service you did not intentionally connect to
- Someone reported seeing unexpected content or behavior
---
"My tool is live and something went wrong."
What to do:
1. If it is Critical severity (data exposed, safety risk, minors affected): Call the Emergency contact now. Do not email. Call.
2. If it is High severity (users blocked, no workaround): Contact the Technical reviewer and consider taking the tool offline.
3. If it is Mediu

title: Vibe Coding Framework
content:
✨ Open Framework v1.0
Vibe Coding Framework
Build AI-powered tools — quickly, safely, and consistently.
VCF guides you through every step of building with AI coding tools
so you don't need to be a developer or memorize every rule.
The framework handles that for you.
---
🚀 How it works
  Answer 5 quick questions about your project. The framework
  picks the right level of guidance.
  Start the intake →
  Your answers map to one of four paths: Quick Build, Standard Build,
  Reviewed Build, or Protected Build.
  Step-by-step instructions while you build.
  The guide teaches you as you go.
  See build guides →
  Quick final check before going live.
  Confirm everything's in order.
  View checklists →
---
  Start with Your First Build — a guided
  walkthrough that takes you through the entire process with a
  simple example project.
  Read When to Escalate — it's
  always okay to ask for help.
---
📦 What's in this framework?
  
    5 questions to classify your project and get your build path
  
  
    What "good" looks like — quality, security, branding, prompts
  
  
    Step-by-step instructions for each build path (learn-by-doing)
  
  
    Quick verification before going live (for experienced builders)
  
  
    Fillable forms for briefs, reviews, incidents, and more
  
  
    First build walkthrough, tool selection, escalation, glossary
  
  
    Your company's tools, roles, branding, and prompt library
  
---
VCF v1.0.0 — Version History

title: Project Intake
content:
Project Intake
Before you build anything, answer these 5 questions. Your answers tell the framework which build path to follow — so you get the right level of guidance without extra steps.
It takes about 2 minutes.
---
The 5 questions
1. Who will use this?
The more vulnerable your audience, the more safeguards you need. An internal dashboard for your team is very different from a tool used by students.
Think about who will actually interact with the finished tool — not who requested it. If a manager asks you to build something that students will use, the answer is "Students or anyone under 18."
---
2. What data does it touch?
Data that identifies real people requires more protection than internal metrics. Protected data (like student records) has legal requirements.
List every piece of information your tool will store, display, or send somewhere. If any of it could identify a real person, select "Personal data." If it includes student records, health information, or financial details, select "Protected data."
---
3. What happens if it breaks or gives wrong information?
A broken prototype is a minor annoyance. A broken tool that gives parents wrong information about their child's progress is a serious problem. The potential damage determines how much testing and review you need.
Imagine the worst realistic thing that could happen if this tool showed wrong data or stopped working for a day. Pick the option that matches that scenario.
---
4. How long will this be used?
A throwaway prototype needs less documentation and review than something your team will rely on every day. Long-lived tools also need maintenance plans.
If you would need more than a day to replace this tool with something else, it is not throwaway. If people would change their daily workflow because it was gone, it is core to operations.
---
5. What role does AI play?
When AI generates content or makes decisions that affect real people, you need extra checks for accuracy, bias, and safety. The more infl

title: Verification Log
content:
Verification Log
Last updated: 2026-03-04
How to read this log
Every factual claim in VCF that references external standards, regulations, tool capabilities, or industry practices is logged here. Each entry records: what was claimed, where it appears, how it was verified, the source URL, and the verification date.
To cross-verify: Click any source link and confirm the claim matches the source. If a source is outdated or contradicts the claim, flag it for the next verification cycle.
---
Regulations and Compliance (R-xxx)
| ID | Claim | Document | Method | Source | Status | Date | Notes |
|----|-------|----------|--------|--------|--------|------|-------|
| R-001 | COPPA applies to children under 13 in the US | security.md, red-protected-build.md | official-docs | ftc.gov/coppa | Verified | 2026-03-04 | US jurisdiction only. 2025 rule amendments add new retention/consent requirements but age threshold unchanged. |
| R-002 | GDPR Article 8 sets age thresholds between 13-16 depending on EU country | security.md, red-protected-build.md | official-docs | gdpr-info.eu/art-8 | Verified | 2026-03-04 | Default is 16; member states may lower to 13. Range confirmed across EU. |
| R-003 | NIST recommends longer passwords over complex ones | security.md | official-docs | NIST SP 800-63B Rev 4 | Verified | 2026-03-04 | NIST eliminates forced complexity requirements; recommends 8 chars minimum, 15+ recommended. |
| R-004 | FERPA protects student education records in the US | glossary.md | official-docs | studentprivacy.ed.gov | Verified | 2026-03-04 | Applies to schools receiving federal funds. |
| R-005 | CCPA grants right to know, delete, and opt out | red-protected-build.md | official-docs | oag.ca.gov/privacy/ccpa | Verified | 2026-03-04 | CPRA amendments (2023) added right to correct and right to limit sensitive data use. |
Tool Capabilities (T-xxx)
| ID | Claim | Document | Method | Source | Status | Date | Notes |
|----|-------|----------|--------|--------|--------|------|-

VCF Version History

Initial release of the Vibe Coding Framework.
Included

Not yet included (planned for future releases)

title: Branding Standard
content:
Branding Standard
Every tool your company puts in front of users represents your brand. AI tools can generate beautiful interfaces — but they default to generic styling. This guide ensures everything looks and sounds like your company.
---
Visual identity
Colors
[CLIENT: Insert your primary, secondary, and accent colors with hex codes]
| Usage | Color | Hex |
|-------|-------|-----|
| Primary (buttons, headers) | [e.g., Navy Blue] | [e.g., #1B3A5C] |
| Secondary (accents, links) | [e.g., Teal] | [e.g., #2ABFBF] |
| Background | [e.g., White] | [e.g., #FFFFFF] |
| Text | [e.g., Dark Gray] | [e.g., #333333] |
| Error/warning | [e.g., Red] | [e.g., #D32F2F] |
| Success | [e.g., Green] | [e.g., #388E3C] |
Consistent colors are the fastest way to make a tool feel "ours." Without defined colors, every AI-generated tool looks different — and none of them look like your company.
1. Open your tool next to your company website or an existing branded product
2. Compare the main colors — buttons, headers, links, backgrounds
3. If the colors do not match the table above, update them before shipping
4. Most AI tools let you set colors in a theme or CSS file. Paste the hex codes from the table.
---
Typography
[CLIENT: Insert font families and sizes]
| Usage | Font | Size |
|-------|------|------|
| Headings | [e.g., Inter Bold] | [e.g., 24px / 20px / 16px] |
| Body text | [e.g., Inter Regular] | [e.g., 16px] |
| Small text / captions | [e.g., Inter Regular] | [e.g., 14px] |
Mismatched fonts make a tool look unfinished. Using your company's standard fonts ties everything together visually.
---
Logo usage
[CLIENT: Insert logo files and usage rules]
- Primary logo file: [link or file name]
- Favicon: [link or file name]
- Minimum clear space: [e.g., equal to the height of the logo mark on all sides]
- Do not stretch, rotate, recolor, or place on busy backgrounds
A misused logo damages brand perception. These rules exist because designers tested what works.
---
Tone of voice
[CLIENT:

title: Prompt Management Standard
content:
Prompt Management Standard
When you build with AI, you give it instructions — prompts. Some prompts are temporary notes you use while building. Others become permanent parts of your tool. This standard explains the difference and how to handle each.
---
Two kinds of prompts
Build prompts (used to create your tool)
What you type into Replit, Cursor, Claude, or any AI tool to generate code. These are your instructions to the AI while building.
Build prompts are temporary. They helped you create the tool, but they are not running inside it.
Production prompts (running inside your tool)
Prompts that your finished tool uses — like a chatbot's system prompt, an AI summarizer's instructions, or a recommendation engine's criteria. These affect what your users see every time they use the tool.
The rule: Build prompts are temporary notes. Production prompts are product decisions.
A build prompt that gives a bad result costs you a few minutes — you regenerate. A production prompt that gives a bad result costs your users' trust, your brand reputation, or worse.
---
Build prompt best practices
1. Start from the prompt library
Check the Prompt Library before writing your own. Tested prompts give better results faster than starting from scratch.
Prompts are like recipes. Starting from a tested recipe and adjusting it is faster and more reliable than inventing one from scratch every time.
2. Be specific about what you want
Vague prompts produce vague results. Specific prompts produce useful results.
Instead of this:
> Make a dashboard
Write this:
> Create a dashboard that shows daily active users, weekly retention rate, and revenue per user. Use a line chart for trends over the past 30 days and summary cards for current values. Use the color scheme: primary #1B3A5C, accent #2ABFBF, background #FFFFFF.
AI tools do exactly what you ask. If you do not specify colors, layout, or data, the AI guesses — and its guesses rarely match your needs.
3. Include your constraints
Tell the AI what

title: Quality Standard
content:
Quality Standard
Not every tool needs to be perfect. But every tool needs to meet a minimum bar before real people use it. This document defines that bar.
---
Universal quality rules (all build paths)
These apply to every project, whether it is a quick prototype or a fully protected tool.
1. It does what it says it does
If your project brief says "show student engagement metrics," it shows student engagement metrics. Not some of them — all of them.
A tool that only partially works creates more confusion than no tool at all. People will assume it is complete and make decisions based on incomplete information.
1. Open your Project Brief
2. Read each item under "What does done look like?"
3. Open your tool and verify each item works
4. If any item does not work, it is not done yet
---
2. It handles mistakes gracefully
If someone enters wrong information or the tool loses its connection, it shows a clear message — not a blank screen or a cryptic error.
When things go wrong (and they will), a helpful message keeps people confident in the tool. A blank screen or "Error 500" makes them stop trusting it entirely.
1. Try submitting an empty form — does it tell you what to fill in?
2. Try entering text where a number is expected — does it explain the problem?
3. Turn off your internet and try using the tool — does it show a clear message?
4. If any of these show a blank screen, a raw error, or no feedback at all, fix it before shipping
---
3. It looks intentional
Consistent colors, aligned elements, readable text. Not polished — intentional. There is a difference between "simple" and "sloppy."
People judge a tool's reliability by how it looks. Misaligned buttons and random font sizes make people question whether the data is reliable too.
1. Look at any two pages in your tool side by side — do they use the same colors, fonts, and button styles?
2. Is all text large enough to read comfortably?
3. Are buttons and links clearly distinguishable from regular text?
4. If anything lo

title: Security Standard
content:
Security Standard
This document defines the security rules for every tool built using VCF. You do not need a technical background to follow these rules.
---
The one rule
Never put sensitive information in your code. Not passwords, not API keys, not secret tokens, not database URLs. Ever.
If sensitive information is in your code, anyone who can see the code can access your accounts, your data, and your users' data. This is the most common security mistake in AI-assisted development — and the easiest to prevent.
1. Open your project in whatever tool you used to build it
2. Use the search function (Ctrl+F on Windows, Cmd+F on Mac)
3. Search for each of these, one at a time: , , , , , 
4. Also search for any long random string that looks like a key or password
5. If you find any of these in your code, move them to environment variables (your tool's settings or secrets panel)
6. Not sure how to move them? See When to Escalate
---
Data handling rules
All build paths
    Every page of your tool should show a lock icon in the browser's address bar. If it does not, your data is being sent unprotected.
    
Without HTTPS, anyone on the same network (a coffee shop, a school) can read the data your tool sends and receives. This includes passwords, personal information, and anything else your users type.
    
1. Open your tool in a browser
    2. Look at the address bar — it should start with  and show a lock icon
    3. If it starts with  (no "s"), your data is not encrypted
    4. Most modern hosting tools (Replit, Vercel, Netlify) provide HTTPS automatically. If yours does not, contact your reviewer.
    If your tool does not need someone's date of birth, do not ask for it. Every piece of data you collect is data you must protect.
    
Data you do not have cannot be stolen. The safest way to protect information is to never collect it in the first place.
    If your tool sends data to an external API, you need to know where that data goes and who has access to it.
    
AI tool
